records.ety: [From Ruff a plaited collar, a drum beat, a tumult: cf. OD. ruyffelen to wrinkle.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Ruffled (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Ruffling (?).]

defs: To make into a ruff; to draw or contract into puckers, plaits, or folds; to wrinkle.
To furnish with ruffles; as, to ruffle a shirt.
To oughen or disturb the surface of; to make uneven by agitation or commotion.
quotes: The fantastic revelries . . . that so often ruffled the placid bosom of the Nile.

defs: To erect in a ruff, as feathers.
quotes: [the swan] ruffles her pure cold plume.

defs: To beat with the ruff or ruffle, as a drum.
To discompose; to agitate; to disturb.
quotes: These ruffle the tranquillity of the mind.
But, ever after, the small violence done Rankled in him and ruffled all his heart.

defs: To throw into disorder or confusion.
quotes: Where best He might the ruffled foe infest.

defs: To throw together in a disorderly manner.
quotes: I ruffled up falen leaves in heap.

records.ety: [Perhaps of different origin from ruffle to wrinkle; cf. OD. roffeln, roffen, to pander, LG. raffein, Dan. ruffer a pimp. Cf. Rufflan.]

defs: To grow rough, boisterous, or turbulent.
quotes: The night comes on, and the bleak winds Do sorely ruffle.

defs: To become disordered; to play loosely; to flutter.
quotes: On his right shoulder his thick mane reclined, Ruffles at speed, and dances in the wind.

defs: To be rough; to jar; to be in contention; hence, to put on airs; to swagger.
quotes: Gallants who ruffled in silk and embroidery.

defs: That which is ruffled; specifically, a strip of lace, cambric, or other fine cloth, plaited or gathered on one edge or in the middle, and used as a trimming; a frill.
A state of being ruffled or disturbed; disturbance; agitation; commotion; as, to put the mind in a ruffle.
A low, vibrating beat of a drum, not so loud as a roll; -- called also ruff.
The connected series of large egg capsules, or oothecae, of any one of several species of American marine gastropods of the genus Fulgur. See Ootheca.
